title: Job Cost Flow — Charging WIP
event: During March, Reyes Co. used $10,000 of direct materials, incurred $8,000 of direct labor, and applied overhead at $8 per direct labor hour on 800 hours. Prepare the entries charging these costs to production.

lines.label: materials requisitioned
lines.why: Direct materials trace straight to specific jobs, so they go straight to WIP.
lines.trap: INDIRECT materials would debit Factory Overhead instead — the requisition slip decides.

lines.label: off the shelf
lines.trap: Credit the inventory account, not an expense — nothing has sold yet.

lines.label: time tickets
lines.why: Direct labor traces to jobs via time tickets.
lines.trap: Indirect labor (supervisors, maintenance) goes to Factory Overhead, not WIP.

lines.label: wages owed
lines.trap: Labor cost is a payable when incurred — cash comes at payday.

lines.label: applied OH
lines.why: Overhead can't be traced, so it rides into WIP on the predetermined rate.
lines.trap: APPLIED overhead, not actual. Actual overhead collects on the other side of the Factory Overhead account.

lines.label: applied out
lines.why: The credit side of Factory Overhead tracks what's been applied to jobs.
lines.trap: Debits to this account are ACTUAL costs; credits are APPLIED — the gap at year-end is over/underapplied.

memorize.body: WIP collects exactly three things: direct materials, direct labor, applied overhead. Everything indirect detours through Factory Overhead.
Factory Overhead is a clearing account: actual in (debits), applied out (credits). It should never appear on the balance sheet.

questions.prompt: DM $10,000, DL $8,000, applied OH $6,400. Total charged to WIP in March = ?

distractors.feedback: You left out applied overhead. WIP carries all THREE product costs — overhead rides in on the predetermined rate.

build.scaffold: Three debits to WIP: materials, labor, applied OH. The credits name where each came from.
